point_001: The core contribution—using fine-tuned or prompted large language models (GPT-2/GPT-3) to generate in-domain unlabeled data—is seen as incremental rather than novel, since both self-training and LLM-based text generation are well-established techniques. The paper lacks a compelling argument for what GAL uniquely contributes beyond combining these known components.
point_002: No comparison is made against alternative methods for obtaining in-domain unlabeled data (e.g., kNN retrieval from large corpora, Wikipedia/NYT text, or generation without fine-tuning), making it difficult to isolate the benefit of the proposed generation approach.
point_003: The paper lacks comparisons with existing data augmentation baselines such as EDA, UDA, and class-conditional generative models, which are directly relevant and widely used. Without these baselines, it is unclear how much of the improvement is attributable to the GAL framework versus simply using more data.
point_004: Most experiments are conducted under full supervision (e.g., MNLI with 400k examples, RTE with thousands of examples), which contradicts the paper's framing of addressing low-resource/few-labeled-data scenarios. The practical motivation for data augmentation is much stronger in genuinely low-resource settings.
point_005: The claim that unconditional autoregressive language models are superior to class-conditional generative models is only partially investigated (Table 6) and not rigorously validated. The observed disadvantage of class-conditional models may stem from noisy pseudo-labels rather than generation quality, and this distinction is not adequately explored.
point_006: The computational cost of combining GPT-2 (or GPT-3) with RoBERTa-large is not discussed, making it difficult to assess the practical feasibility and cost-benefit trade-off of the proposed framework.
point_007: The paper does not include standard deviations or significance tests for results in Tables 1 and 2, making it hard to determine whether small performance differences are statistically meaningful.
point_008: The rationale for the tabular data encoding strategy is insufficiently explained, and there is no analysis of why XGBoost outperforms the proposed method on two of the four tabular datasets. Comparing against XGBoost is also arguably misleading given the large remaining performance gaps.
point_009: Table 5 lacks a no-GAL baseline, making it difficult to directly quantify the improvement attributable to the proposed framework in the few-shot setting.
point_010: The paper claims 'consistent improvements with more GAL iterations' in Table 2, but results for MNLI and RTE do not clearly support this claim, indicating an overstatement.
point_011: Metric labels are missing or unclear in several tables (e.g., the slash-separated scores for MNLI, MRPC, STS-B, QQP are not explained), and Table 6 only covers a subset of GLUE tasks without justification.
point_012: Section 5.3 on synthetic data quality would benefit from a human-curated or oracle-quality data experiment to better understand the ceiling of the approach and the limitations imposed by current generative models.
point_013: There is a potential anonymization breach: the GAL entry on the public GLUE leaderboard is associated with an identifiable name, which may compromise double-blind review integrity.
